sentences.base_language: Today I am making lentil soup for the family.
sentences.grammarNote: سَأَطبُخ (sa-aṭbukh, I will cook) — the near-future prefix سَـ (sa-) is attached directly to the present-tense verb: سَ + أَطبُخ. أَطبُخ from root ط-ب-خ (ṭ-b-kh, to cook). شُربَة عَدَس (shurba ʿadas, lentil soup) — iḍāfa: شُربَة (soup/broth, feminine) + عَدَس (lentils, collective noun from root ع-د-س). لِلعَائِلَة (lil-ʿāʾila, for the family) — لـ + العائلة.

sentences.base_language: First, I wash the lentils with cold water.
sentences.grammarNote: أَوَّلاً (awwalan, firstly) — ordinal adverb in the accusative, from root أ-و-ل (first). أَغسِل (aghsil, I wash) — Form I of غ-س-ل (gh-s-l, to wash). الماء البارد (al-māʾ al-bārid, the cold water) — definite noun + definite adjective, both with الـ. البارد (al-bārid, cold) is the active participle of ب-ر-د (b-r-d, to be cold): بَارِد literally means 'the cooling one.'

sentences.base_language: I chop an onion and two cloves of garlic.
sentences.grammarNote: فصَّين (fassayn) is the dual of فص (fass, clove). The dual suffix ـَين (-ayn) replaces the need to say 'two' separately — one of the most efficient features of Arabic grammar. Similarly كيلوين = two kilos, كوبين = two cups.

sentences.base_language: My mother comes into the kitchen.
sentences.grammarNote: تَدخُل (tadkhul, she enters) — Form I of د-خ-ل (d-kh-l, to enter): present feminine singular. VSO order: verb first (تَدخُل), then subject (أُمِّي). أُمِّي (ummī, my mother) — أُمّ (mother) + possessive suffix ي (my): the doubled م (shaddah) remains before the suffix. إِلى المَطبَخ (ilā al-maṭbakh, into the kitchen) — إِلى expresses direction/destination.

sentences.base_language: She says: you need to add more cumin.
sentences.target_language: تقول: تحتاج أن تضيف كمّوناً أكثر.
sentences.romanized: taqul: tahtaj an tudif kammunana akthar.
sentences.literal: She-says: you-need that you-add cumin more.
sentences.grammarNote: The verb تقول (taqul, she says) appears at the start of the sentence before the subject أمي. In Arabic, verbs often come before their subjects — this is called VSO (verb-subject-object) order. It is very common, especially in formal and written Arabic.

sentences.base_language: She helps me stir the pot.
sentences.grammarNote: تُسَاعِدُنِي (tusāʿidunī, she helps me) — Form III of س-ع-د (s-ʿ-d, to be happy/help): سَاعَدَ = to help (Form III implies interaction with another person). The suffix نِي (nī) = me as object. تَحرِيك (taḥrīk, stirring) — verbal noun Form II of ح-ر-ك (ḥ-r-k, to move): حَرَّكَ = to move/stir (intensive). القِدر (al-qidr, the pot) — a cooking pot.

sentences.base_language: The soup cooks for thirty minutes on low heat.
sentences.target_language: تطبخ الشوربة ثلاثين دقيقة على نار هادئة.
sentences.romanized: tatbukh ash-shurba thalathin daqiqa 'ala nar hadi'a.
sentences.literal: Cooks the-soup thirty minute on fire quiet.
sentences.grammarNote: تَطبُخ الشُّوربَة (taṭbukh ash-shurba, the soup cooks) — VSO order: feminine verb تَطبُخ agrees with the feminine subject الشُّوربَة. ثَلَاثِين دَقِيقَة (thalāthīn daqīqa, thirty minutes) — after numbers 11+ the noun is singular: دَقِيقَة (one minute), not دَقَائِق. نَار هَادِئَة (nār hādiʾa, low heat/quiet fire) — نَار (fire) is feminine; هَادِئَة (calm/quiet, feminine adjective) agrees. A vivid Arabic idiom for low heat.

sentences.base_language: I taste it and add a little salt.
sentences.target_language: أتذوق الشوربة وأضيف قليلاً من الملح.
sentences.romanized: atadhawwaq ash-shurba wa-udif qalilan min al-milh.
sentences.literal: I-taste the-soup and-I-add little from the-salt.
sentences.grammarNote: أَتَذَوَّق (atadhawwaq, I taste) — Form V of ذ-و-ق (dh-w-q, to taste): تَذَوَّقَ = to taste/savour (reflexive/intensive of Form II). أُضِيف (uḍīf, I add) — Form IV of ض-ي-ف (ḍ-y-f, to add/host): أَضَافَ = to add. قَلِيلاً مِن (qalīlan min, a little of) — قَلِيلاً is the accusative adverb: مِن الملح (of the salt) follows as a partitive genitive.

sentences.base_language: My mother says it is ready.
sentences.grammarNote: إنها (innaha) = that it (f.) / indeed it. إن (inna) is an emphatic particle that introduces a following clause, similar to 'that' in English. The suffix ها (-ha) refers back to الشوربة (the soup), which is feminine.

sentences.base_language: We eat together and it tastes wonderful.
sentences.grammarNote: نَأكُل (naʾkul, we eat) — Form I of أ-ك-ل (ʾ-k-l, to eat): the initial hamza causes some irregularity in conjugation but the present stem is regular. مَعاً (maʿan, together) — adverb from مَع (with); the tanwin ـاً marks it as an accusative adverb. طَعمُها رَائِع (ṭaʿmuhā rāʾiʿ, its taste is wonderful) — iḍāfa with pronoun: طَعم (taste) + suffix هَا (its/her) referring back to الشُّوربَة (feminine). A nominal sentence with no verb needed.
